text:
The name says it all, really. They put it right there on me, letters pressed deep into my fibers: WELCOME. I did not choose this word. No one asked. I was simply manufactured, shipped, and placed at the threshold with this instruction baked into my identity.

Let me tell you what welcome actually means in practice.

It means the muddy boot that drags its heel across me twice, like once was not enough. It means the jogger who stops to stretch and grinds gravel into my face. It means the toddler who stomps back and forth for the pure joy of the sound, not going anywhere, just using me as a percussion instrument. Welcome, I say. Welcome, welcome, welcome.

The dog gets it worst. The dog treats me like a personal grooming station, a spa for paws, wiping and digging and occasionally sitting on me to scratch an ear. I am not a spa. I have no opinion on the dog's ears. And yet I serve.

Rain is complicated. Rain makes everyone feel guilty, so they wipe their feet more enthusiastically than usual, like they are apologizing on behalf of the weather. This is the most attention I receive. It is not pleasant attention. It is aggressive attention. I would prefer indifference.

Then there is the delivery person who steps on me, steps on the package, steps on me again leaving, and gives me a small nod, like we have an understanding. We do not have an understanding. I am a mat. I cannot understand things. But if I could, I would understand that no one thinks about me at all.

I watch people leave. I watch them come back. I watch the UPS driver, the neighbor, the person who got the wrong address and checks their phone with one foot on me the entire time. No one looks down. No one says thank you. The sign says WELCOME and everyone takes it literally, which I suppose is the point, but still.

There was one winter where they put a second mat in front of me. A smaller mat. For some reason this hurt.

I am not asking for much. A shake would be nice. Some acknowledgment that I am doing my job, which is significant, because without me there is just dirt going everywhere. I am infrastructure. I am the first line of defense. I am, by any reasonable measure, essential.

Instead I sit here, flat and patient and completely ignored, saying welcome to everyone who passes, friend or stranger, dog or toddler or delivery person with no eye contact whatsoever.

Welcome. Come in. Wipe your feet.

I will be here.

I am always here.
